items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: The first sentence matches the approved translation and preserves the mock-heroic register. However, the line is duplicated in the actual subtitles (appears twice back-to-back), which is not in the source and harms timing/comic rhythm. Also, the reference analysis notes that «У Шпака — …, у посла — …» implies loss/association rather than current possession; the chosen English phrasing is acceptable as a shorthand in context, and since it is identical to the reference, it should not be penalized on that point.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. It preserves the intended humor: Soviet-era stiff politeness and repetitive self-introduction clashing with the royal setting and Bunsha’s drunken impersonation.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Keeps the abrupt authoritarian performative force and comic escalation.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Preserves the archaic/high-register melancholy and the comic mismatch with the modern setting.

items.translation: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Keeps the subject assignment (“the demons” as agents) and the paranoid-mystical misreading of technology.

items.translation: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Retains the mock-biblical flourish and the joke’s ideological edge (religious certainty vs. mundane mechanics).

items.translation: The floor is open to everyone!
items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Captures the emcee/MC vibe and the mass-participation command, fitting the scene’s anachronistic party energy.

items.translation: Now, this was a good call!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Preserves the breezy self-satisfaction and the “lucky find” tone that makes the thief charming.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Correctly avoids calling the eggplant spread “caviar,” preserving the Soviet-status-symbol inversion joke and preventing confusion.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Keeps the comic counting cadence, the status-item list, and the self-incriminating stumble on the jacket count.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Preserves the calm, sing-song fatalism that turns a frightening reality (psychiatric ward) into a shared absurdist punchline.

summary.interpretation: 0.45 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.09 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
